inline_review:
commit 78266633b402721e6fd40df54378f55bda9106a9
Author: David Rientjes <rientjes@google.com>

mm, page_alloc: reintroduce page allocation stall warning

This commit reintroduces a warning for page allocations that stall longer
than 10 seconds to help debug userspace unresponsiveness. It limits the
warning to fire at most once every 10 seconds system-wide.

> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -4693,6 +4701,40 @@ check_retry_cpuset(int cpuset_mems_cookie, struct alloc_context *ac)
>  	return false;
>  }
>  
> +static void check_alloc_stall_warn(gfp_t gfp_mask, nodemask_t *nodemask,
> +				unsigned int order, unsigned long alloc_start_time)
> +{
> +	static DEFINE_SPINLOCK(alloc_stall_lock);
> +	unsigned long stall_msecs = jiffies_to_msecs(jiffies - alloc_start_time);
> +
> +	if (likely(stall_msecs < ALLOC_STALL_WARN_MSECS))
> +		return;
> +	if (time_is_after_jiffies(READ_ONCE(alloc_stall_warn_jiffies)))
> +		return;

Will this check evaluate incorrectly on 32-bit systems after a long period
without stalls?

On 32-bit systems, if no allocation stall occurs for more than LONG_MAX
jiffies (about 24.8 days at HZ=1000), jiffies will advance far beyond
alloc_stall_warn_jiffies. At that point, the signed difference inside
time_is_after_jiffies() wraps and becomes negative, causing it to return
true.

Does this result in the warning being silently suppressed for the
subsequent 24 days until the distance wraps back into the positive half
of the timeline?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"The suppression timestamp `alloc_stall_warn_jiffies` is subject to a 24-day wrap-around bug on 32-bit systems, which will silently disable the stall warning for long periods.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. `time_is_after_jiffies(a)` is defined as `time_after(a, jiffies)`, which is implemented as `(long)(jiffies - a) < 0`.
2. On 32-bit systems, `long` is 32 bits. If the system does not experience any allocation stalls for more than `LONG_MAX` jiffies (approximately 24.8 days at HZ=1000), `jiffies` will advance more than `LONG_MAX` past `alloc_stall_warn_jiffies`.
3. At this point, `jiffies - alloc_stall_warn_jiffies` will exceed `LONG_MAX`. When cast to a signed `long`, this value becomes negative, meaning `time_is_after_jiffies()` will incorrectly evaluate to `true`.
4. Consequently, `check_alloc_stall_warn()` will erroneously return early, silencing any legitimate page allocation stall warnings for the next 24.8 days.
5. While this doesn't cause a crash or data loss, it defeats the purpose of the newly reintroduced diagnostic warning on 32-bit architectures, warranting a Medium severity."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 78266633b402721e6fd40df54378f55bda9106a9
Author: David Rientjes <rientjes@google.com>

mm, page_alloc: reintroduce page allocation stall warning

This commit reintroduces a warning for page allocations that stall longer
than 10 seconds to help debug userspace unresponsiveness. It limits the
warning to fire at most once every 10 seconds system-wide.

> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -4693,6 +4701,40 @@ check_retry_cpuset(int cpuset_mems_cookie, struct alloc_context *ac)
>  	return false;
>  }
>  
> +static void check_alloc_stall_warn(gfp_t gfp_mask, nodemask_t *nodemask,
> +				unsigned int order, unsigned long alloc_start_time)
> +{
> +	static DEFINE_SPINLOCK(alloc_stall_lock);
> +	unsigned long stall_msecs = jiffies_to_msecs(jiffies - alloc_start_time);
> +
> +	if (likely(stall_msecs < ALLOC_STALL_WARN_MSECS))
> +		return;
> +	if (time_is_after_jiffies(READ_ONCE(alloc_stall_warn_jiffies)))
> +		return;

Will this check evaluate incorrectly on 32-bit systems after a long period
without stalls?

On 32-bit systems, if no allocation stall occurs for more than LONG_MAX
jiffies (about 24.8 days at HZ=1000), jiffies will advance far beyond
alloc_stall_warn_jiffies. At that point, the signed difference inside
time_is_after_jiffies() wraps and becomes negative, causing it to return
true.

Does this result in the warning being silently suppressed for the
subsequent 24 days until the distance wraps back into the positive half
of the timeline?

"}
